Q1 — Place: An old colonial-era post office in the town I grew up in. High ceilings, fans that didn't work, and these absurd proportions — a single tiny window placed exactly off-center on a giant blank wall. I'd go in to mail letters with my father and just stare up. The room knew it was being looked at. The disused balcony on the second floor was set back from the railing exactly the wrong distance — so people walking on it appeared and disappeared like figures on a stage. I never thought of it as architecture. I just thought the building was strange in a deliberate way.
Q2 — Habit: I sketch floor plans of rooms I'm in. Not formally — on receipts, napkin corners, the back of meeting agendas. I'll be in a restaurant and the layout will be bothering me, so I'll redraw it with the bar moved three feet, the kitchen entrance reoriented, a column removed. I also can't enter a building without noticing which doors people are actively avoiding. There's almost always one door — usually slightly off-axis, or placed near a transition — that people refuse to use. It tells you something about how the architect thought, or didn't.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain why a poorly-designed building exhausts you to be in. Most people blame the lighting or the air. What's actually happening is that the proportions of the spaces don't match the implicit movement patterns your body wants to make. If a corridor is six inches too narrow for two people to pass without ducking, you spend the entire walk anticipating contact. If a ceiling is twelve inches lower than your peripheral vision expects, your shoulders hold tension. You don't notice any of these individually. They accumulate. By the third hour your body has been making micro-adjustments to the geometry of the room continuously, and you're depleted.
Q4 — Conflict: I had a fight with the contractor renovating my parents' apartment about the placement of a single doorway. He wanted to put it on the wall closest to the kitchen — practical, shortest plumbing run. I insisted it had to go on the opposite wall, even though it cost more. The surface story was budget. The real story was that the kitchen-side placement would have forced anyone entering the room to immediately face the back of the fridge as their first visual. A door's location decides what the room reveals first. He thought I was being precious. I knew that detail would make the apartment feel wrong forever.
Q5 — Sound: The sound of a footstep in a building tells you the volume of the space before you turn the corner. I learned this as a kid by being slightly afraid of basements. A footstep in a normal-height room has a particular near-immediate reflection; in a basement with a low ceiling, the reflection is faster and tighter; in a stairwell, it stretches. You can map a building by listening to your own steps. I do this without meaning to. When I walk into a hotel, I know whether the lobby is 9 feet high or 14 feet high before I look up.
Q6 — Body: I was at an art gallery and my body stopped at the threshold of one specific room. My mind hadn't even processed what was in front of me. Some quality of that room — proportion, light, distance to the opposite wall — had triggered an actual physical hesitation, like I was about to step into a different temperature. I forced myself in. The room held a single sculpture in the exact spot where my body had said 'wait.' The architect had built that pause into the geometry. I stood there with my heart going for about ten minutes.
Q7 — Unworded: The feeling of a space that is RIGHT. Not beautiful — right. There's a particular proportion at which a ceiling height and a floor area and the placement of one window relate to each other so that walking into the room makes your body settle. You don't think 'this is nice.' Your body just lowers its shoulders. Most rooms don't have it. The ones that do are usually old, or designed by someone obsessed. I have spent my life trying to identify the actual ratio. I think it's not one ratio — it's a relationship — but I've never found the words for it.
